name: Fundraising Funnel
summary: A structured approach to political fundraising — from broad awareness to small donations to major donor cultivation, all within Australian electoral law.
whenToUseIt: When setting up a campaign fundraising operation, when planning a fundraising event, when building a donor database, or when transitioning from grassroots to major donor fundraising.
steps: Top of funnel: Build awareness — social media, community events, earned media coverage
Capture contacts: Email list, event attendees, social media followers — always with clear consent
First ask: Small-dollar digital donation ($5-$50) with a compelling reason to give now
Nurture: Regular updates showing impact — 'Your $20 funded 100 door-knocks this weekend'
Upgrade: Identify recurring small donors and invite to mid-tier events ($100-$500)
Major donors: Personal cultivation — one-on-one meetings, private briefings, policy discussions ($1,000+)
Compliance: Track every donation, ensure all disclosures meet AEC requirements, maintain donor privacy
example: A first-time candidate in a federal electorate might begin fundraising 18 months before the election. Month 1-6: build an email list of 2,000 through community events and social media. Month 6-9: launch a small-dollar campaign raising $30,000 from 800 donors (average $37.50). Month 9-12: host two fundraising events ($200/head, 100 attendees each = $40,000). Month 12-18: cultivate 10 major donors at $5,000-$10,000 each = $50,000-$100,000. Total raised: $120,000-$170,000, which is competitive for an independent candidate. All donations above the disclosure threshold ($16,300 in 2024-25) must be reported to the AEC.
